summary: Joel Spolsky curates highlights from his 'Ask Joel' forum, sharing his opinions on a wide range of topics including publishing, Microsoft's program manager role, Lisp, software methodologies, API design, startup costs, and software pricing. The post is a collection of colorful, opinionated snippets rather than a single cohesive argument. Each excerpt showcases Spolsky's signature irreverent style and willingness to deliver blunt takes on industry topics. The selections reveal his pragmatic worldview: that human factors, business realities, and practical constraints matter more than theoretical ideals in software.
key_points: Program managers secretly hold decision-making power over developers because they have superior people skills, even though both groups believe they're in charge.
Despite respecting Lisp and Paul Graham, ignoring the fact that millions of programmers learned Lisp and chose not to use it constitutes 'morbid cognitive dissonance.'
Enterprise software pricing has a dead zone between $3,000 and $100,000 where sales costs make the business model unworkable.
Big-M methodologies like RUP are obsessed with business objects and payroll systems, making them a poor fit for consumer-facing software development.
API usability should conform to the user's mental model (one-based indexing) even when the technically superior choice (zero-based) exists.
money_quotes: Developers couldn't people-skill their way out of a summer intern party at BillG's lakeside mansion.
If you try to ignore the fact that millions of programmers around the world have learned lisp and don't prefer to use it, you're in the land of morbid cognitive dissonance.
That's why you see a lot of software products at $100,000 and a lot under $3000, but anywhere in-between and it's impossible to make sales.
Indexes are one based. That's how humans count. Zero-based is better, I agree, but one-based is what humans expect, and the program model must conform to the user model for ease of use.

Everything about RUP, for example, is obsessed with figuring out what the business objects and business rules are so you can do a payroll system. We do things like add spell checkers to an editor window.
The law firm that was recommended to us was big and famous and wanted a $30,000 retainer just to talk to us.
Teach me about women's shoes and I will feign interest and then promptly forget everything you told me.
key_insight: Practical human factors — people skills, mental models, pricing psychology, and real-world adoption — consistently trump theoretical technical superiority in software.
